---
title: "Weekly Report — Week 5 (20.03.2026 – 26.03.2026)"
output:github_document
date: "2026-03-20"
---

## Summary

This week focused on redesigning the simulation framework to enable
multi-seed averaging and snapshot-based metric tracking, and on running
the first systematic parameter study under the new setup. The core question
was how responsiveness to power affects delegation behaviour, representation
quality, and vote loss across different network structures — now estimated
robustly over 100 independent seeds.

---

## Work Done

**Simulation Framework Redesign**

- Replaced single-seed runs with a 100-seed averaging framework to remove
  seed-specific noise from all outcome estimates
- Moved from varying T as a parameter to a snapshot-based approach:
  metrics are now recorded at T = 50, 100, 150, and 200 within a single
  T = 200 run, which is both more efficient and captures the true temporal
  trajectory of a single simulation rather than four independent ones
- Added per-round history tracking for cheap metrics (`lost_vote_rate`,
  `avg_drift`, `delegation_rate`, `delegation_stability`) enabling full
  time series plots in addition to snapshot comparisons
- Added snapshot-level tracking for expensive metrics (`gini_power`,
  `top5_power_share`, `avg_chain_length_delegators`,
  `largest_voting_bloc_share`, `delegation_stability`) at 25%, 50%,
  75%, and 100% of T
- Implemented parallel execution via `furrr::future_pmap()` to keep
  runtime manageable at 100 seeds

**Model Extension — Inertia Parameter**

- Added `inertia` as a retention probability parameter to
  `simulate_liquid_democracy()` with default `inertia = 0` (fully
  backward compatible)
- At `inertia = 0` agents reconsider their full delegation every round
  — memoryless baseline
- At `inertia > 0` agents retain their current delegate with probability
  `inertia` before evaluating alternatives, producing more stable
  delegation structures
- Parameter is scale-invariant with respect to `responsiveness` and
  directly interpretable as a probability

---

## Experiment 3 — Responsiveness to Power (Multi-Seed)

### Design

| Parameter | Values |
|---|---|
| Responsiveness | 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 25 |
| Rewiring probability (p) | 0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.25 |
| Seeds | 1 – 100 |
| Community | 1 community, 250 lay agents |
| T | 200 (snapshots at 50, 100, 150, 200) |
| Inertia | 0 (memoryless baseline) |

### Network

A single Watts-Strogatz small-world network of 250 lay agents with
`node_degree = 6`. Each agent is initially connected to their 3 nearest
neighbours on each side in a ring topology. Edges are then rewired with
probability `p_rewire` to random lay agents across the full network,
introducing long-range shortcuts. All friendship edges are bidirectional —
any agent can delegate to any of their neighbours. The network is fixed
for the duration of each simulation; only the delegation graph changes
each round.

### Key Results

**Ideological Drift**
Average ideological drift decreases consistently with higher responsiveness
across all rewiring conditions and snapshot timepoints, from roughly
0.20–0.23 at responsiveness = 0.1 to around 0.14–0.16 at responsiveness
= 25. The effect flattens beyond responsiveness ≈ 5. Results are
substantially more stable than in Experiment 1 due to 100-seed averaging
— the variance across rewiring conditions is visibly reduced.

**Lost Vote Rate**
Lost vote rate drops steeply with responsiveness, from ~0.35 at
responsiveness = 0.1 to ~0.05–0.10 at responsiveness ≥ 5. The pattern
is consistent across all four snapshot timepoints, confirming that the
stochastic equilibrium is reached quickly and T has little additional
effect.

**Delegation Rate**
Delegation rate decreases monotonically with responsiveness from ~0.80
to ~0.60, consistent with Experiment 1. The dual role of the
responsiveness parameter — inflating both neighbour attractiveness and
the self-weight — drives agents toward direct voting as responsiveness
increases.

**Delegation Stability**
Delegation stability fluctuates around a stable mean of approximately
0.20–0.35 across all conditions and never approaches the 99% convergence
threshold. This confirms the memoryless baseline does not produce
structural convergence — the system reaches a stochastic equilibrium
rather than a fixed delegation structure.

### Summary

The multi-seed results confirm and stabilise the findings from Experiment 1.
Higher responsiveness consistently improves representation quality and
reduces vote loss. The system does not structurally converge under the
memoryless baseline regardless of T, rewiring, or responsiveness level.

---

## Open Issues
